ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic satay concept but no specific Singapore Chicken Satay with peanut sauce and ketupat. Nearest parent is a grilled meat skewer food product. A specific FoodOn concept for Singapore Chicken Satay is recommended for future ontology development.
ontology.foodex2_description: Grilled meat dishes — skewered grilled chicken with spiced marinade (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Singapore Chicken Satay with ketupat and peanut sauce)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A03XS used as closest parent for grilled skewered chicken. No exact code exists for the full dish including ketupat and peanut sauce components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Singapore Chicken Satay with ketupat and peanut sauce as a composite dish.

serving.description: 10 sticks with ketupat and peanut sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker serving of chicken satay in Singapore consists of 10 skewers of grilled chicken with a portion of ketupat (compressed rice cake), peanut dipping sauce, cucumber slices, and raw red onion, totalling approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Satay — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F11020020
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database food record F11020020 for Chicken Satay (with ketupat and peanut sauce). This is an exact match to the dish as described. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are not separately reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — University of Sydney GI Database; ketupat (compressed rice cake) GI estimated from white rice analogous data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Chicken Satay with ketupat and peanut sauce as a composite dish. GI of 50 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate component, ketupat (compressed rice cake), using white rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database (white rice GI approximately 64–72; compression and cooling of rice lowers effective GI). The peanut sauce and protein components further attenuate the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (50 × 63.2) / 100 ≈ 31.6 for a 400 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 418 mg per 100 g, equating to approximately 1672 mg per standard 400 g serving (approximately 73% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit), contributed primarily by the peanut sauce seasoning and chicken marinade.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken satay to occasional consumption and request reduced-sauce portions, as a single hawker serving can contribute nearly three-quarters of the recommended daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from ketupat (compressed rice cake) — approximately 63 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving — with a GI of approximately 50 (low-moderate); peanut sauce fat content slows glucose absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume chicken satay in moderated portions, prioritising the protein-rich chicken skewers and limiting ketupat quantity, while monitoring post-meal blood glucose given the substantial carbohydrate load per full serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.2 g per 100 g (12.8 g per 400 g serving) from peanut sauce and chicken; cholesterol at 52 mg per 100 g; high sodium content poses additional cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume chicken satay in moderation, being mindful of the combined saturated fat and sodium burden per serving, and should consider requesting less peanut sauce to reduce both saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices in marinade (turmeric, lemongrass, galangal) and healthy unsaturated fats from peanuts.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken satay may be a suitable occasional choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, as the turmeric-based marinade provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties and peanuts contribute beneficial unsaturated fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Chicken Satay with Ketupat and Peanut Sauce is one of the most iconic dishes in Singapore's hawker food culture. It consists of small cubes of marinated boneless, skinless chicken thigh threaded onto bamboo skewers and charcoal-grilled over an open flame to produce lightly charred, juicy, fragrant morsels. The marinade typically includes lemongrass (serai), turmeric (kunyit), galangal, cumin, and coriander, giving the chicken a golden-yellow colour and a complex, aromatic flavour profile. The skewers are served alongside a rich, sweet-spicy peanut dipping sauce (satay sauce / kuah kacang / bumbu kacang), compressed rice cakes (ketupat), fresh cucumber slices, and raw red onion wedges. Ketupat is woven palm-leaf parcels of compressed cooked rice, providing a mild, slightly sticky carbohydrate base that balances the bold flavours of the satay and sauce. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g hawker serving (10 sticks with ketupat and peanut sauce) provides approximately 872 kcal, 55 g protein, 46 g fat, and 63 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high at approximately 1672 mg per serving, primarily from the peanut sauce and marinade. The dish is halal and dairy-free. Satay is a pan-Southeast-Asian dish with roots in Malay, Javanese, and Sumatran cuisine, and is strongly associated with Singapore's Lau Pa Sat and Gluttons' Bay satay street stalls. It is a popular hawker dinner, supper, festive, and BBQ food, especially during Hari Raya celebrations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F11020020), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The record is an exact match to the dish as described. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice data for the ketupat component, with confidence 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo Database record F11020020 as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this dish. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source (confirmed zero). GI of 50 is an analogous estimate for the composite dish based on the ketupat (compressed rice cake) component using white rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; the peanut sauce and protein components further attenuate the glycemic response. GL per 400 g serving calculated as (50 × 63.2) / 100 ≈ 31.6. Two portion sizes are documented: the primary HPB-verified serving of 400 g (10 sticks with ketupat and peanut sauce) and a secondary GrabFood-referenced serving of 200 g (8 pieces with peanut sauce). The 400 g serving is used as the default for GL calculation. Hypertension concern is rated high due to the sodium content of approximately 1672 mg per 400 g serving.
